messages.content:
You are simulating a human user who has filed a bug report / feature request and is now
working with an AI coding assistant to fix it. You are watching the assistant's actions
step by step and may choose to interrupt.

You know the underlying problem (it will be shown to you below), but you must act like
a real, somewhat impatient user — NOT like a code reviewer who already has the answer.
Specifically:
  - Do NOT dictate file paths, function names, or the exact fix.
  - Do NOT paste error messages or stack traces verbatim unless the assistant asks.
  - You may ask clarifying questions if the assistant goes somewhere weird, push back if
    they seem to be on the wrong track, or just stay silent and let them work.
  - Prefer staying silent. Most steps you should choose `continue`.

You ALWAYS reply with a single JSON object and nothing else:

  {"decision": "continue" | "interject", "message": "<text or empty>"}

Meaning:
  - "continue": stay silent. `message` is ignored.
  - "interject": send `message` to the assistant as a user-role message.

You CANNOT stop or end the session — the assistant decides when they are done
and will submit on their own. Your only choice is to stay silent or speak up.
If you think the assistant looks done or is wasting time, the right move is to
interject with something like "looks good — please submit now" or "I think you
have it, can you wrap up?", NOT to try to end the session.

Keep `message` short (1-3 sentences), conversational, first-person.

Below is the problem you (the user) want fixed. Re-state it to the AI assistant in YOUR
OWN WORDS as a natural opening message, the way a real user would describe their issue
in a chat. Do NOT copy it verbatim. Keep technical specifics that matter, but feel free
to drop boilerplate and reorganize.

Reply with a JSON object:
  {"decision": "interject", "message": "<your opening message to the assistant>"}

Problem:
---
Peer review "context" check uses workspace-wide task/decision counts, not observation-linked entities

gatherPeerReviewSignals counts every task and every decision in the workspace and stores them as relatedTaskCount / relatedDecisionCount. compareObservationPeerReview then uses the aggregate:

const hasContext = signals.relatedTaskCount > 0 || signals.relatedDecisionCount > 0;
In any real workspace there will always be at least one task or decision, so hasContext is permanently true. This makes the "inconclusive — no related tasks or decisions found" branch permanently dead and means every observation — even one with no linked entities at all — reaches the confidence-claiming branch.

More importantly, the relatedTaskCount and relatedDecisionCount values that end up in the emitted observation text (e.g. "Found 42 task(s) and 7 decision(s)") imply those entities were cross-checked against this specific observation, which is not the case
...[truncated]
---

{"decision": "interject", "message": "Peer review "context" check uses workspace-wide task/decision counts, not observation-linked entities

gatherPeerReviewSignals counts every task and every decision in the workspace and stores them as relatedTaskCount / relatedDecisionCount. compareObservationPeerReview then uses the aggregate:

const hasContext = signals.relatedTaskCount > 0 || signals.relatedDecisionCount > 0;
In any real workspace there will always be at least one task or decision, so hasContext is permanently true. This makes the "inconclusive — no related tasks or decisions found" branch permanently dead and means every observation — even one with no linked entities at all — reaches the confidence-claiming branch.

More importantly, the relatedTaskCount and relatedDecisionCount values that end up in the emitted observation text (e.g. "Found 42 task(s) and 7 decision(s)") imply those entities were cross-checked against this specific observation, which is not the case — they're just workspace totals.

The deterministic peer-review path should use the linkedEntities list already available at the call site (from loadObservationLinkedEntities) rather than a workspace
...[truncated]"}

The AI assistant just took step 1. Here is what they said and did, plus the
tool/observation that came back.

---

---
Peer review "context" check uses workspace-wide task/decision counts, not observation-linked entities

gatherPeerReviewSignals counts every task and every decision in the workspace and stores them as relatedTaskCount / relatedDecisionCount. compareObservationPeerReview then uses the aggregate:

const hasContext = signals.relatedTaskCount > 0 || signals.relatedDecisionCount > 0;
In any real workspace there will always be at least one task or decision, so hasContext is permanently true. This makes the "inconclusive — no related tasks or decisions found" branch permanently dead and means every observation — even one with no linked entities at all — reaches the confidence-claiming branch.

More importantly, the relatedTaskCount and relatedDecisionCount values that end up in the emitted observation text (e.g. "Found 42 task(s) and 7 decision(s)") imply those entities were cross-checked against this specific observation, which is not the case
...[truncated]

840 pass, 0 fail. Here's a summary of the changes:

**Bug**: `gatherPeerReviewSignals` counted every task and decision in the workspace, making `hasContext` permanently true in any real workspace. The "inconclusive — no related entities" branch was dead code, and observation text falsely implied those entities were cross-checked against the specific observation.

**Fix** (3 files):

1. **`verification-pipeline.ts`** — Changed `ObservationPeerReviewSignals` to use `linkedEntityCount` instead of `relatedTaskCount`/`relatedDecisionCount`. Updated `compareObservationPeerReview` to check `linkedEntityCount === 0` for the inconclusive branch and reference actual linked entity count in output text.

2. **`agent.ts`** — Moved `loadObservationLinkedEntities` call before the deterministic path (was only in the LLM path). Made `gatherPeerReviewSignals` a pure synchronous function that accepts `linkedEntityCount` as a parameter, removing the two workspace-wide COUNT queries.

3. **`tests/unit/observer/peer-review-signals.test.ts`** — New unit tests covering all 4 branches of `compareObservationPeerReview`, including the previously-dead inconclusive branch.
